extracted_information: Google Docs utilizes Operational Transform (OT) for its real-time collaborative editing architecture to ensure consistency, low latency, and concurrency handling. OT allows multiple users to make edits, which are then represented as operations and sent to a central server (Google's sync service). This server orders and transforms operations to maintain consistency, sending the transformed operations back to all users. An example demonstrates how OT transforms operations (e.g., Alice typing 'Hello' and Bob typing 'World') to prevent conflicts and ensure the correct output ('HelloWorld'). Mathematically, it ensures `T(A,B) = T(B,A)` for consistency.

how_it_works: Each user’s changes are represented as operations.
Operations are sent to a central server (Google’s sync service).
The server orders and transforms operations to maintain consistency.
Transformed operations are sent back to all users.
operational_transform_mechanism.example: Alice types 'Hello' at position 0, Bob types 'World' at position 5. OT transforms Bob's operation to ensure 'HelloWorld' appears, not 'WorldHello'.

features.name: Operational Transform (OT) advantages for Google Docs
details: Works well for structured text editing

features.name: Operational Transform (OT) limitations
details: Requires a central server for coordination
Harder to implement for decentralized collaboration

crdt_performance.sync_complexity: Each user syncs with 999 other users
crdt_performance.memory_usage: Higher due to multiple stored versions
